title: Vehicle trapped by rising floodwater
summary: Recognize the need to abandon an immobilized vehicle early and move away from water without attempting a crossing.
content:
Recognize the need to abandon an immobilized vehicle early and move away from water without attempting a crossing.

Action sequence: Never drive into a flooded road, torrent, underpass, or moving water. If the vehicle becomes immobilized and water is rising, call 112, release seat belts, help children or dependent passengers, and leave when a safe exit is possible. Move to stable higher ground away from the water path, bridges, banks, and fallen wires. Report the exact vehicle location and number of people; do not return for property. Safety boundary: Do not enter moving floodwater on foot to reach a road or vehicle. Do not stay inside merely because the vehicle feels heavy or protected.
materials: seat-belt cutter and window tool stored accessibly
phone. Context: “Vehicle trapped by rising floodwater”.
steps: Never drive into a flooded road, torrent, underpass, or moving water.

Move to stable higher ground away from the water path, bridges, banks, and fallen wires.
Report the exact vehicle location and number of people; do not return for property.
warnings: Do not enter moving floodwater on foot to reach a road or vehicle.
Do not stay inside merely because the vehicle feels heavy or protected.

title: Stranded vehicle in snow or frost
summary: Conserve heat, maintain ventilation, make the vehicle visible, and avoid carbon monoxide exposure while arranging rescue.
content:
Conserve heat, maintain ventilation, make the vehicle visible, and avoid carbon monoxide exposure while arranging rescue.

Action sequence: Call for help with road, direction, landmarks, vehicle description, occupants, fuel, and medical needs. Stay with the vehicle unless a clearly visible safe building is very close and authorities advise movement. If the engine is used, first ensure the exhaust outlet remains completely clear and maintain safe ventilation; use short intervals to conserve fuel. Layer clothing, keep people dry, move fingers and toes gently, and check each other for confusion or worsening cold stress. Safety boundary: Do not sleep with the engine running continuously. Do not walk into poor visibility, traffic, or unfamiliar terrain without a confirmed safe destination.

title: Wildfire evacuation vehicle loading order
summary: Load people, essential medicines, communication, animals, documents, and water in a fixed order so departure is not delayed by possessions.
content:
Load people, essential medicines, communication, animals, documents, and water in a fixed order so departure is not delayed by possessions.

Action sequence: Park facing the escape direction when risk rises and keep enough fuel or charge for the planned route and diversion. Load dependent people and mobility aids first, then medicines, communication, animal control, identity documents, and water. Leave at the earliest official instruction or when the route, visibility, smoke, or household ability begins to deteriorate. Use the official route and keep windows closed and ventilation set to recirculate when smoke is present, if the vehicle system allows it. Safety boundary: Do not delay departure to load furniture, tools, fuel containers, or replaceable property. Do not drive toward smoke or fire to inspect it or retrieve another vehicle.

Pedestrian evacuation route with no private vehicle

title: Pedestrian evacuation route with no private vehicle
summary: Plan realistic walking, public-transport, neighbour, taxi, municipal, and responder options for households without a car.
content:
Plan realistic walking, public-transport, neighbour, taxi, municipal, and responder options for households without a car.

Action sequence: Mark two walkable routes that avoid torrents, dense vegetation, tunnels, steep unstable slopes, and roads without safe pedestrian space. Identify bus stops, taxi contacts, neighbours, municipal assistance, accessible transport, and safe pickup points before an alert. Set a lower departure threshold because walking and assisted transport take longer than driving. Carry only what can be moved safely while keeping hands available for balance, children, or mobility aids. Safety boundary: Do not choose a route simply because it is shortest; hazard exposure and accessibility matter more. Do not wait until smoke, water, darkness, or crowding makes assisted transport impossible.

title: Vehicle emergency kit for Greek summer heat
summary: Build a heat-safe vehicle kit and storage routine that does not leave water, medicines, batteries, or people in dangerous temperatures.
content:
Build a heat-safe vehicle kit and storage routine that does not leave water, medicines, batteries, or people in dangerous temperatures.

Action sequence: Keep a permanent non-medical kit, but bring temperature-sensitive medicines, power banks, and drinking water from indoors for each trip. Plan fuel or charge, shaded stops, roadside assistance, and a contact who knows the route. If stranded, move occupants to the safest cool location available, use shade and airflow, and call for help before symptoms worsen. Inspect containers, batteries, adhesives, food, and plastic items after prolonged heat exposure and replace damaged stock. Safety boundary: Never leave a child, dependent adult, or animal in a parked vehicle, even briefly. Do not store medicines or swollen, leaking, or damaged batteries in a hot vehicle.
materials: water carried fresh for each trip
